What Are Smart Textiles?
========================

Smart textiles weave tech into traditional fabrics, collecting data about the wearer and their immediate surroundings.

![Image 4: Brooke Becher](https://cdn.builtin.com/cdn-cgi/image/f=auto,w=96,h=96,q=100/https://builtin.com/sites/www.builtin.com/files/2022-09/BecherBrooke_SquareHeadshot_AuthorArchive%20%281%29.jpeg)

 Written by [Brooke Becher](https://builtin.com/authors/brooke-becher)

Published on Nov. 25, 2024

[](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https%3A%2F%2Fbuiltin.com%2Farticles%2Fsmart-textiles&mini=true)

![Image 5: Smart Textiles](https://cdn.builtin.com/cdn-cgi/image/f=auto,fit=cover,w=320,h=200,q=80/https://builtin.com/sites/www.builtin.com/files/2024-11/smart-textile.jpg)

Image: Shutterstock

Smart textiles are wearable fabrics embedded with electronic components, like sensors and actuators, that react to their environment. They can measure stimuli, like temperature, moisture, pressure, light and sound, and respond to an input through dynamic behaviors like changing color or shape, releasing heat or delivering [haptic feedback](https://builtin.com/hardware/haptic-technology).

What Are Smart Textiles?
------------------------

Smart textiles refers to materials with embedded electronics — such as sensors, actuators or conductive fibers — that enable them to detect and respond to external stimuli.

There are two main types of smart textiles: active and passive. Active smart textiles can interact with their environment and respond to stimuli, while passive ones can only monitor these changes without altering their behavior​.

Smart textiles can be used for health monitoring of patients, performance tracking of athletes and operational support of first responders.

An introduction to smart textiles. | Video: Steven the Engineer

How Do Smart Textiles Work?
---------------------------

Smart textiles use specialized fibers, such as conductive yarns, piezoelectric materials or temperature-sensitive fabrics to sense and actuate. With built-in sensors, a smart textile can measure a variety of metrics, from heart rate and body temperature to how much force is being applied to the fabric and what chemicals are in the wearer’s immediate environment.

Once this data is collected, it’s then transmitted to a processing unit. This step can take place within the microprocessors of a wearable device, or outsourced to a smartphone or a connected app, where the information is translated into actionable insights. For example, a device that tracks a runner’s physical activity may relay information to an app that provides feedback on the wearer’s pace, stride or footstriking pattern. An on-body EKG monitor can continually track a patient’s cardiac rhythm, detecting irregularities in the wearer's heart beat. And so on.

If the smart textile is active, the fabric can respond to the data it gathers by triggering a physical change. This could be anything from adjusting temperature, like [heating](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1385894724025270?dgcid=rss_sd_all) or [cooling down](https://news.uchicago.edu/story/uchicago-researchers-invent-new-fabric-reduces-heat) a jacket, or [changing color based on UV exposure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1385894724045571).

Through this combination of sensing, processing and actuation, smart textiles offer a wide array of applications with their ability to track a wearer’s movements, physiology and surroundings.

Related Reading[Exoskeleton Suits: Real-Life Examples](https://builtin.com/robotics/exoskeleton-suit)

Applications of Smart Textiles
------------------------------

More than just a fashion statement, smart textiles are used across a wide range of real-word applications.

### Healthcare

Smart textiles are used to deliver [more personalized diagnostics](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41928-022-00723-z), monitoring a patient’s vital signs, like heart and respiration rate, with biosensors. Over time, they can track physiological information to flag abnormalities and even catch early warning signs for conditions such as arrhythmias or irregular breathing patterns.

### Sport and Fitness

Smart textiles enable precise monitoring of athletic performance, measuring key metrics like heart rate, muscle activity and range of motion to provide actionable insights. Embedded into [full-body swim suits](https://www.techradar.com/news/this-high-tech-swimsuit-boosts-speed-by-replicating-the-texture-of-a-whales-belly), [yoga pants](https://www.wearablex.com/) and app-connected footwear, water-resistant e-textiles use motion sensors to collect data and analyze biomechanics. This information can then be used to enhance performance or even prevent injuries.

### Military

In the military sector, e-textiles [can be used](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/346311696_Smart_Textiles_for_Defence_Applications) to monitor vital signs, detect injuries, locate soldiers and transmit real-time data to medical and intel teams, as well as detect environmental hazards, such as toxic gasses or radiation, for optimal situational awareness.

### Workplace Safety

Workers wearing biosensors in their garments can be monitored for heat stress or fatigue, while motion sensors can be used to collect data for injury prevention in industries like construction or logistics. Additionally, smart uniforms with location-tracking features enhance on-the-job safety for first responders and those working in particularly hazardous conditions, like miners, enabling rapid response to emergencies.

Examples of Smart Textiles
--------------------------

### Hexoskin’s Smart ‘Astroskin’ Shirt

Astronaut David Saint-Jacques explains how this smart shirt monitors his vitals in space. | Video: Canadian Space Agency

This [washable headband and shirt set](https://hexoskin.com/pages/astroskin-vital-signs-monitoring-platform-for-advanced-research) is built with a heart monitor, breath sensors, a pulse oximeter and skin temperature sensors that support real-time biometric monitoring for up to 48 hours at a time. Originally developed for astronauts, Hexoskin’s Astroskin can detect everything from cardiac conditions to sleep quality of the user, while studying the effects of altitude on the body during space missions.

### Nuubo’s Wearable EKG Monitor

A walkthrough on how Nuubo’s EKG monitor helps diagnose cardiac patients. | Video: Xantus Viomedical

Medtech company Nuubo has developed [a cardiac monitoring system](https://www.nuubo.com/en-us/patients/what-is-nuubo) in the form of a vest that can be worn under clothes for up to 30 days. Embedded with vital-tracking electrodes, it records cardiac rhythms continuously during daily activities. Any anomalies are detected quickly, then documented in a report that informs a patient’s treatment upon their next office visit.

### 3DKnITS, an Intelligent Sock by MIT

MIT’s smart sock thermoforms to the user when it senses pressure. | Video: MIT Media Lab

MIT researchers [developed a smart fabric](https://www.media.mit.edu/projects/3dknits/overview/) using advanced knitting machines to create customizable, body-conforming textiles. As the user applies different levels of pressure, sensors in the 3D-shaping fabrics collect information about a user’s biomechanics — like their gait, balance and muscle coordination — for AI-enabled analysis.

### Feel Sound with CuteCircuit’s SoundShirt

This wearable creates a full body sensory experience for hard-of-hearing users. | Video: Cooper Hewitt

The [SoundShirt](https://cutecircuit.com/soundshirt/) is a garment developed by CuteCircuit that allows deaf users to “feel” music. With 28 micro-actuators and haptic sensors integrated into its fabric, the shirt translates sound into vibrations that are felt on the skin, mapping different sound frequencies to various parts of the body. This creates an immersive experience that makes musical performances more inclusive and engaging for those with hearing impairments.

### UCR's Self-Healing Fabric

The self-repairing properties of this material were inspired by Marvel’s comic book character Wolverine. | Video: UCR

Researchers at the University of California, Riverside [created a conductive, self-healing fabric](https://www.universityofcalifornia.edu/news/wolverine-inspired-material) that autonomously repairs itself. After being cut, the material can re-attach itself in under 24 hours at room temperature. Made up of ionic conductors, the material can be electrically activated, and could be used to power artificial hearts and robots.

### Wearable X’s Smart Leggings

These yoga pants keep users in proper form by sending vibrational cues. | Video: Dezeen

Fashion tech company Wearable X launched its line of [Nadi X smart yoga pants](https://www.wearablex.com/pages/how-it-works), woven with built-in sensors and haptic feedback, to guide a user’s yoga practice. The smart leggings work alongside a mobile app to provide vibrational cues that prompt the wearer to adjust their form, enhancing their yoga flow with instruction of proper alignment.

Frequently Asked Questions
--------------------------

### What are examples of smart fabrics?

Examples of smart fabrics include moisture-wicking athletic wear, temperature-regulating materials, color-changing garments as well as textiles with embedded sensors, like Hexoskin’s biometric monitoring headband and shirt set or MIT’s AI-enabled footwear.

### What is the difference between smart textiles and e-textiles?

A smart textile is an umbrella term that encompasses any fabric with functional properties that respond to external stimuli, whereas an e-textile, which is a type of smart textile, refers to materials that are specifically integrated with electronic components, like sensors or circuits.

# Sensory interactive fibers and textiles

[*npj Flexible Electronics*](/npjflexelectron)
**volume 9**, Article number: 23 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

8218 Accesses

1 Citations

2 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Electronic textiles (e-textiles) have gradually emerged as a burgeoning industry, with the advancement of flexible electronic technology and the growing demand for personalization, convenience, and comfort. As the typical representative, sensory interactive e-textiles, integrated with visual, auditory, tactile, and other sensory experiences, have garnered significant attention in the next generation of wearable devices due to their outstanding performance and unique immersive interactive experience. To promote the practical application and better development of sensory interactive e-textiles, this paper reviews the research status of sensory interactive fibers and textiles in recent years, providing a detailed overview of functional fibers capable of achieving sensory interactive functions, categorizes system integration technologies for sensory interactive e-textiles, and summarizes the application scenarios of sensory interactive e-textiles. This review further delineates current design paradigms of e-textiles and proposes a novel design paradigm applicable to sensory interactive e-textiles. Finally, we clarify the challenges facing the future development of sensory interactive e-textiles and suggest vital research directions.

## Introduction

Textile apparel is considered a second skin for people, primarily offering essential protection functions[1](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR1 "Fernández-Caramés, T. & Fraga-Lamas, P. Towards the internet-of-smart-clothing: a review on IoT wearables and garments for creating intelligent connected e-textiles. Electronics 7, 405 (2018)."). Additionally, textile garments reflect personal aesthetic preferences. In the recent decade, they have been given new functions as intelligent textiles. Intelligent textiles contain electronic fibers in various textile structures for transmission and detection, housing intelligence functions such as sensing, actuation, and communication. Especially, intelligent interactive e-textiles convert stimuli from the environment (temperature, light, chemicals, moisture, pH) or interactions (mechanical force and electromagnetic field) into responses in aesthetic (color, light intensity, fluorescence, shape or form) or physical (mechanical, electrical, thermal, chemical, wetting or moisture transport) properties[2](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR2 "Zeng, W. et al. Fiber-based wearable electronics: a review of materials, fabrication, devices, and applications. Adv. Mater. 26, 5310–5336 (2014)."). These textiles engage almost all human senses, such as visual, haptic, auditory, or olfactory. Hence, sensory interactive e-textiles are indispensable in human-machine interaction due to their unique dynamic change characteristics, providing a more intelligent and personalized wearing experience. Sensory interactive e-textiles have great potential in diversified fields, including personal protection[3](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR3 "Fang, Y., Chen, G., Bick, M. & Chen, J. Smart textiles for personalized thermoregulation. Chem. Soc. Rev. 50, 9357–9374 (2021)."), sports[4](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR4 "Tang, J., Wu, Y., Ma, S., Yan, T. & Pan, Z. Flexible strain sensor based on CNT/TPU composite nanofiber yarn for smart sports bandage. Compos. Part B Eng. 232, 109605 (2022)."), healthcare[5](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR5 "Yang, X. et al. Textile fiber optic microbend sensor used for heartbeat and respiration monitoring. IEEE Sens. J. 15, 757–761 (2015)."), military[6](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR6 "Revaiah, R. G., Kotresh, T. M. & Kandasubramanian, B. Technical textiles for military applications. J. Text. Inst. 111, 273–308 (2020)."), and fashion[7](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR7 "Ruckdashel, R. R., Venkataraman, D. & Park, J. H. Smart textiles: a toolkit to fashion the future. J. Appl. Phys. 129, 130903 (2021)."). Despite their significant advancements, developing and applying sensory interactive e-textiles exhibit considerable growth potential. More importantly, how to create sensory interactive e-textiles that meet user needs and achieve the best interactive performance is still an area to be explored.

Compared to traditional materials, the uniqueness of sensory interactive e-textiles is highly multidisciplinary and collaborative. Developing and incorporating sensory interactive e-textiles need contributions from seemingly unrelated fields, such as material scientists, computer scientists, artists, and designers[8](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR8 "Coulter, J. The designers leap: boundary jumping to foster interdisciplinarity between textile design and science. J. Text. Des. Res. Pract. 6, 1–26 (2018)."). They are united in exploring concepts for intelligent e-textiles yet separated in their approach. On the one hand, scientists discover new materials and characterize their properties, while engineers apply a material’s properties and functionalities to solve problems. On the other hand, designers and artists move materials out of the science lab and into practical applications[9](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR9 "Posch, I. & Kurbak, E. CRAFTED LOGIC Towards Hand-Crafting a Computer. In Proc. 2016 CHI Conference Extended Abstracts on Human Factors in Computing Systems 3881–3884 (Association for Computing Machinery). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1145/2851581.2891101
                  
                 (2016)."). These collaborations, while enabling the innovation of sensory interactive e-textiles, also present challenges to address gaps brought at every step, from manufacturing each component to the end-use application.

This review defines the three typical development generations of intelligent e-textiles. Firstly, the sensory interactive e-textiles based on different senses have been summarized better to understand the intelligent interaction between humans and textiles. Then, detailed insights into how flexible electronics have been or can be incorporated into e-textile systems. Meanwhile, considering the characteristics of multidisciplinary cooperation, we propose a new design paradigm based on analyzing the inadequacy of the existing intelligent e-textile design process. Lastly, the development trend and potential problems of sensory interactive e-textiles are highlighted. Thus, this review aims to help scientists and designers collaborate more effectively in developing sensory interactive e-textiles in the context of interdisciplinary work. At the same time, envision the potential contribution of innovative interactive e-textile technologies for revolutionizing human-machine interaction interface in the information society, aiming to catalyze further iterative progress in the field of next-generation sensory interactive e-textiles.

### The history of interactive e-textiles

Interactive e-textiles have evolved significantly, progressing through three generations: (1) rigid electronics on a textile platform, (2) devices embedded in textiles, and (3) fully textile devices. The three generations’ development of interactive e-textiles is shown in Fig.[1](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#Fig1). The foundational advances in electronics, catalyzed by the invention of the transistor in the late 1940s, facilitated the integration of electronic components into textiles. In 1968, Designer Diana Dew[10](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR10 "Smart Textiles: Fundamentals, Design, and Interaction (Springer International Publishing, Cham). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-50124-6
                  
                 (2017).") created a wearable Pop Art piece, “Movie Dress”, a luminescent textile that laminated thin phosphor lights onto the leather. Movie Dress brings a new way of wearing and entertaining people. The first generation of e-textiles pushed the boundaries of textile craftsmanship and engineering by constantly experimenting with embedded electronics. Therefore, they were also called “smart textiles-adapted”, which means that the textiles do not encompass electronic functions and electronic components are combined with textiles in the form of attachments. These early e-textiles were cumbersome and lacked the aesthetics, textural comfort, bending, and drapability typical of textiles.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41528-025-00398-4/MediaObjects/41528_2025_398_Fig1_HTML.png)

A three-generation development of interactive e-textiles.

In the 1990s, the second generation of interactive e-textiles emerged, marked by the development of components such as textile antennas[11](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR11 "Sankaralingam, S. & Gupta, B. Determination of dielectric constant of fabric materials and their use as substrates for design and development of antennas for wearable applications. IEEE Trans. Instrum. Meas. 59, 3122–3130 (2010)."), semiconductors[12](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR12 "De Rossi, D. A logical step. Nat. Mater. 6, 328–329 (2007)."), electrodes[13](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR13 "Xu, P. J., Zhang, H. & Tao, X. M. Textile-structured electrodes for electrocardiogram. Text. Prog. 40, 183–213 (2008).") and sensors[14](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR14 "Castano, L. M. & Flatau, A. B. Smart fabric sensors and e-textile technologies: a review. Smart Mater. Struc. 23, 053001 (2014)."). Textile technology and new integration techniques such as weaving, knitting, surface coating, electroplating, embroidery, printing, and lamination were applied to functional materials[15](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR15 "Seyedin, S. et al. Fibre electronics: towards scaled-up manufacturing of integrated e-textile systems. Nanoscale 13, 12818–12847 (2021)."). Therefore, the second generation of e-textiles is called “smart textiles-integrated”. This category of textiles covers some electronic functions, utilizing new integration techniques to establish interrelationships between electronic components and textiles. Conductive and semiconductive materials such as fibers, inks, films, and gels combine with traditional yarns or fabrics to create specific structures and patterns that generate electrical, mechanical, magnetic, and optical properties. Second generation e-textiles, developed from functional yarns using integration techniques, effectively address challenges related to comfort, flexibility, tactile properties, and washability[16](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR16 "Uz Zaman, S., Tao, X., Cochrane, C. & Koncar, V. Launderability of conductive polymer yarns used for connections of e-textile modules: mechanical stresses. Fibers Polym. 20, 2355–2366 (2019)."). These e-textiles have been applied in fashion aesthetics, biometric monitoring and therapeutic interventions[17](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR17 "Shen, J., Chui, C. & Tao, X. Luminous fabric devices for wearable low-level light therapy. Biomed. Opt. Express 4, 2925–2937 (2013)."). However, second generation e-textiles can usually only be used to solve a single or limited problem, and their interaction with the human body is restricted. Therefore, third generation e-textiles with integrated multi-functionality have been developed.

The third generation of e-textiles involves embedding microelectronic devices directly into the fibers. Since the early 21st century, microconductors, semiconductors, and insulators have been embedded in individual fibers. They can utilize complex computing power to provide intelligent functions such as visual and auditory processing, sensing, and communication[18](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR18 "Bernardo, C. P. C. V., Lameirinhas, R. A. M., de Melo Cunha, J. P. & Torres, J. P. N. A revision of the semiconductor theory from history to applications. Discov. Appl. Sci. 6, 316 (2024)."). Stylios et al.[19](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR19 "Stylios, G. K. & Chen, M. The Concept of Psychotextiles; interactions between changing patterns and the human visual brain, by a Novel Composite SMART Fabric. Materials 13, 725 (2020)."). have engineered an innovative electrochromic composite yarn. The composite yarn consists of a core thread and an outer sheath, which are thermochromic materials that change color at a given temperature. When an electric current travels through the yarn’s core, it generates heat, triggering the outer sheath’s color transition and affecting visual change. The functionality of third generation e-textiles tends more towards improvements at the fiber level, enabling fibers to carry electronic functions. Hence, these textiles are termed “smart textiles-based”, encompassing electronic capabilities. Textile-based innovative technologies represent a significant advancement in textile functionality. Ideal innovative fabrics directly incorporate electronic components and connections into the textile structure so that the electronic systems can be freed from rigidity and encapsulation and can be closely in our daily lives. This approach allows to offer discreet and comfortable wearable options. Furthermore, third generation e-textiles possess advanced capabilities, including multidimensional computing power, which are crucial for developing soft interfaces for Artificial Intelligence, Internet of Things, and 5 G wireless technologies[20](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR20 "Cleary, F., Srisa-An, W., Henshall, D. C. & Balasubramaniam, S. Emerging AI technologies inspiring the next generation of E-Textiles. IEEE Access 11, 56494–56508 (2023).").

Currently, the third generation of e-textiles utilizes advanced functional materials, sensing technology, and embedded systems to integrate electronic components with textiles. These textiles can detect changes in the environment, user actions or physical conditions and respond accordingly to enhance the user experience through increased intelligence, personalization and comfort. Therefore, the third generation of e-textiles is also called intelligent interactive e-textiles. E-textiles are important in human-machine interaction, which offer substantial potential for creating innovative and natural interfaces for non-invasive intelligent living applications. This advancement helps narrow the connectivity gap between humans and machines, facilitating a more intuitive human-machine interface. The technology seamlessly integrates into flexible fabrics, enhancing the user experience and usability, enabling more effective interaction during everyday activities.

### The typical representative of sensory interactive e-textiles

Under the current trend of technological development, the integration of flexible electronics based on textile substrates have inspired extensive attention and in-depth exploration in the field of academic research due to their extraordinary potential in the field of wearable technology, especially their unique value in driving smart garments to achieve a high degree of interactivity. By integrating electronic functions into traditional textiles, e-textiles not only broaden the application boundaries of bright clothing but also provide new possibilities for intelligent interaction between clothing, individuals, and the environment, signifying that innovative textile technology is moving towards a more humane and integrated advanced stage.

Intelligent interactive e-textile, as a product of the profound fusion of textile technology and information technology, integrates various types of textile-based materials to build an interactive system. Fiber and textile-based functional materials give e-textiles unique interactive characteristics and functional properties. By dynamically adjusting the physical state of the fabric, such as morphology change, color transformation, temperature regulation, or vibration generation, these functional materials greatly enhance the fabric’s functionality and significantly improve the user’s interactive experience. Sensory interaction is crucial for enhancing the interactive experience between humans and clothing, as shown in Fig. [2](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#Fig2). We have categorized the e-textile according to different sensory interaction modes. The approach not only gains a deeper understanding of how various materials affect the sensory experience but also guides us to design more highly interactive e-textiles, which can further enhance the user experience.

Sensory Interaction in Human-Clothing Interaction.

### The visual interaction e-textiles

Vision plays a vital role in all senses and is the most active sensory channel for transmitting information (~83%) in human-machine interaction. People can create colorful patterns to express their emotions through simple but imaginative combinations of color and light. In recent years, the rapid development of flexible electronic materials has led to the emergence of highly flexible and dynamically transformable smart textiles as a new interaction interface. As a result, adaptable, intelligent, luminescent, and chromatic materials have become a topic of considerable interest and have been widely studied. According to the different mechanisms, illuminated devices can be divided into photoluminescent, electroluminescent, and mechanoluminescent. Similarly, chromatic devices can be divided into electrochromic, thermochromic, and photochromic colored devices. This section will introduce the latest research on fiber-shaped illuminated and chromatic materials.

### Luminescent

Luminescence has fascinated humans since immemorial, thanks to natural phenomena such as aurora borealis, lightning, and luminous animals[21](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR21 "Armaroli, N. & Bolink, H. J. Photoluminescent Materials and Electroluminescent Devices (Springer Cham, 2016)."). Depending on the source of excitation, for example, electromagnetic radiation, stress (mechanical force), electric current, temperature, or magnetic field, different types of luminescence can be observed, such as photoluminescence (PL)[22](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR22 "Eliseeva, S. V. & Bünzli, J.-C. G. Lanthanide luminescence for functional materials and bio-sciences. Chem. Soc. Rev. 39, 189–227 (2010)."), mechanoluminescence (ML)[23](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR23 "Zhuang, Y. & Xie, R.-J. Mechanoluminescence rebrightening the prospects of stress sensing: a review. Adv. Mater. 33, e2005925 (2021)."), electroluminescence (EL)[24](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR24 "Friend, R. H. et al. Electroluminescence in conjugated polymers. Nature 397, 121–128 (1999)."), thermoluminescence (TL)[25](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR25 "Chen, R. & McKeever, S. W. S. Theory of Thermoluminescence and Related Phenomena. (WORLD SCIENTIFIC). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1142/2781
                  
                 (1997)."), and magnetic-induced luminescence (MIL)[26](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR26 "Wong, M.-C., Chen, L., Tsang, M.-K., Zhang, Y. & Hao, J. Magnetic-induced luminescence from flexible composite laminates by coupling magnetic field to piezophotonic effect. Adv. Mater. 27, 4488–4495 (2015)."), respectively. Light, electricity, and mechanics—affording PL, EL, and ML, respectively—are by far the most utilized, and this part is specifically dedicated to these three fibers.

*Photoluminescence (PL)* is the emission of radiation induced by the optical excitation of a sample using an external light source. Existing photoluminescent materials include organic fluorophores, quantum dots, perovskites, polymer dots, lanthanide-doped nanoparticles, and so forth[27](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR27 "Lyu, P. et al. A straightforward approach for high-level anticounterfeiting and antibacterial applications based on photoluminescent Fibers/Yarns. Adv. Mater. Technol. 6, 2100302 (2021)."). Over the past years, photoluminescent materials have attracted great attention in the textile industry due to intriguing applications in anticounterfeiting, optical sensors, smart haute couture, and wearable electronic devices[28](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR28 "Yang, C. et al. Ionotronic luminescent fibers, fabrics, and other configurations. Adv. Mater. 32, 2005545 (2020)."),[29](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR29 "Medina-Castillo, A. L., Fernández-Sánchez, J. F. & Fernández-Gutiérrez, A. One-step fabrication of multifunctional core-shell fibres by co-electrospinning. Adv. Funct. Mater. 21, 3488–3495 (2011)."). Generally, luminescent fibers can be fabricated by spinning polymer matrices with fluorescent materials incorporated or coating fluorescent materials onto the surface of fibers. Presently, several protocols have been used for fabricating fluorescent fibers, including in situ growth[30](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR30 "Wang, X., Gao, W., Xu, S. & Xu, W. Luminescent fibers: in situ synthesis of silver nanoclusters on silk via ultraviolet light-induced reduction and their antibacterial activity. Chem. Eng. J. 210, 585–589 (2012)."), dry-wet spinning method, co-electrospinning and multistep dip-coating[31](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR31 "Ding, W. et al. Stretchable multi-luminescent fibers with AIEgens. J. Mater. Chem. C. 7, 10769–10776 (2019)."). Due to their flexibility and bendability, the luminescent fibers can be integrated with conventional yarns using various textile fabrication techniques, such as weaving and knitting, to create luminescent fabrics. Bai et al.[32](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR32 "Bai, Z., Tan, J., Johnston, C. F. & Tao, X.-M. Connexion: development of interactive soft furnishings with polymeric optical fibre (POF) textiles. Int. J. Cloth. Sci. Tech. 27, 870–894 (2015).") developed an artificial intelligence fiber-optic fabric that integrates computer vision and deep learning. This fabric can change light and color modes in response to non-contact body postures, facilitating user interactive behaviors. To improve the practicability and interactivity of photoluminescent fibers, Li et al.[33](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR33 "Li, R. et al. Structural coloration and up/down-conversion photoluminescence of carbon nanotube fibers for ultraviolet detection. Adv. Opt. Mater. 12, 2400282 (2024)."). introduced silane-functionalized carbon dots (SiCDs) into silica PCs and achieved structural coloring and PL of CNT fibers (CNTFs), as shown in Fig. [3a](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#Fig3). The structural coloration and up/down conversion PL of CNTFs were realized by coating CNTFs with SiCDs@SiPCs. The structural color was generated by silica PCs coated on the surfaces of CNTFs through a simple self-assembly method. The fabrication of SiCDs@SiPCs-CNTFs not only realizes the coloration of CNTFs but also exhibits new PL properties, which play important roles in visual interactive e-textiles, flexible devices, and so on.

According to the different light-emitting materials and principles, the *electroluminescence (EL)* fibers can be broadly divided into organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs), polymer light-emitting diodes (PLEDs), polymer luminescent electrochemical cells (PLECs), and alternating current electroluminescence (ACEL) systems and so on[34](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR34 "Yu, X. et al. Structural design of light-emitting fibers and fabrics for wearable and smart devices. Sci. Bull. 69, 2439–2455 (2024)."). One approach to flexibilization is to incorporate stiff light-emitting diode (LED) directly-based light-emitting (LE) devices into flexible fibers and textiles[35](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR35 "Rein, M. et al. Diode fibres for fabric-based optical communications. Nature 560, 214–218 (2018)."),[36](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR36 "Hardy, D. A. et al. Engineering a costume for performance using illuminated LED-yarns. Fibers 6, 35 (2018)."). In these systems, tiny LE elements are embedded within the fiber or attached to the surface of the fiber/fabric to inherit the intrinsic flexibility of the fiber. Another method is to sequentially deposit the functional layers of the two-dimensional (2D) planar LE device onto the fiber substrate, obtaining LE fibers with a coaxial structure[37](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR37 "Liang, J., Li, L., Niu, X., Yu, Z. & Pei, Q. Elastomeric polymer light-emitting devices and displays. Nat. Photon. 7, 817–824 (2013)."). This method provides excellent mechanical flexibility and high luminous efficiency. Additionally, Stretchable electroluminescent (EL) devices have recently received increasing attention as input and/or output interfaces for next-generation human-friendly visual electronic systems. Choi et al.[38](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR38 "Yoo, J., Ha, S., Lee, G. H., Kim, Y. & Choi, M. K. Stretchable high-resolution user-interactive synesthesia displays for visual–acoustic encryption. Adv. Funct. Mater. 33, 2302473 (2023).") present recent advances in materials and design strategies for stretchable EL devices. They mainly discussed two representative strategies to realize stretchable EL devices: “structural approach by using stretchable device designs” and “material approach by using intrinsically stretchable electronic/optoelectronic materials for device fabrication”. There have been a large amount of research efforts to advance stretchable EL fibers in response to the growing need for high-performance visual interaction. Chun et al.[39](/articles/s41528-025-00398-4#ref-CR39 "Chun, F. et al. Multicolour stretchable perovskite electroluminescent devices for user-interactive displays. Nat. Photon. 18, 856–863 (2024).") developed a novel class of EL phosphors consisting of PeNPs coated with ZnS (PeZS), which electrically excites the PeNPs through total intra-particle energy transfer. Abstract
--------

Next generation textiles (NGTs) represent a paradigm shift in the textile industry, incorporating the most recent advances in materials, technologies, and functionalities. The dynamic world of textiles is undergoing an extraordinary transition, ushering in the age of NGTs. This article discusses the mechanisms, difficulties, present advancements, and potential future opportunities associated with these NGTs. This assessment traverses the domains of nanotechnology, [3D printing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/three-dimensional-printing "Learn more about 3D printing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), recycling, [wearable electronics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wearable-electronics "Learn more about wearable electronics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), machine learning, [biomimicry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biomimetics "Learn more about biomimicry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [energy harvesting](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/energy-harvesting "Learn more about energy harvesting from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") while focusing on sustainability, functionality, smart integration, [advanced manufacturing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/advanced-manufacturing "Learn more about advanced manufacturing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") techniques, and [multifunctionality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/multifunctionality "Learn more about multifunctionality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as major factors. The paper highlights the wonderful prospects of improved performance, sustainability, [intelligent textiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/intelligent-textile "Learn more about intelligent textiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [wearable technologies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wearable-technology "Learn more about wearable technologies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), highlighting consumerization, personalization, safety, and protection enhancements. Furthermore, a key method for overcoming major challenges is discussed which is to adopt open innovation, which encourages collaboration, knowledge exchange, and the integration of external resources, all of which aid in addressing scalability and mass production, costing, and a lack of modern technologies in the NGTs industry.

1. Introduction
---------------

Textiles are positioned to play a significant part in determining the future of fashion, sustainability, and functionality as technology develops and consumer needs change ([Fouda et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib33), [Farooq and Zhang, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib31)). It needs to be more functional, comfortable, responsive as well as sustainable ([Persson et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib81)). The initial purpose of textiles was to protect people from the rain and cold. Later in history, clothes began to incorporate aesthetic considerations as well ([Mostafizur Rahman et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib69)). In recent years, smart and interactive textiles have been introduced as a new generation of [textile fabrics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/textile-fabric "Learn more about textile fabrics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Syduzzaman et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib108)). Next generation textiles (NGTs) represent a paradigm shift in the textile industry, incorporating the most recent advances in materials, technologies, and functionalities. These textiles strive to meet modern demands for sustainability, advanced performance, smart capabilities, and versatility, putting them at the forefront of innovation in the [textile and apparel industries](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/textile-and-apparel-industry "Learn more about textile and apparel industries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). These textiles go beyond traditional fabrics, incorporating aspects like [smart sensors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/smart-sensors "Learn more about smart sensors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), functionality, responsive materials, sustainable components, and functional coatings ([Castano and Flatau, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib12)). Moreover, the EU took a wonderful initiative to form a strategy that aims to make all textiles sustainable ([Commission, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib21)).

In recent years, NGTs have become more and more dependent on nanotechnology. Nanotechnology has been used to create fabrics and garments with sustainable antibacterial, UV-resistant, electrically conductive, optical, hydrophobic, and flame-retardant properties ([Hossain et al., 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib42), [Hossain, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib41)). A wide [range of functions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/range-of-function "Learn more about range of functions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), including [energy harvesting](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/energy-harvesting "Learn more about energy harvesting from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and storage, sensing, drug release, and optics, are now being performed by smart textile-based nanomaterial-based systems. In addition to finding considerable use in the fashion industry, these technologies are being expanded for wider utilization in military, healthcare, and on-body energy harnessing applications ([Shah et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib98)). Another technique is [3D printing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/three-dimensional-printing "Learn more about 3D printing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which can play a wide role in the case of next gen textiles. [3D printing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/3d-printing "Learn more about 3D printing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can be used as stretchable elastic fibers ([Chen et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib15)), and [thermoplastic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/thermoplastics "Learn more about thermoplastic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[monofilament](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/monofilament "Learn more about monofilament from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for [smart textile](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/smart-textile "Learn more about smart textile from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which is flexible and conductive ([Eutionnat-Diffo et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib30)). An article showed about bio-fabricated textile fabrics that were derived from algae, fungi, and other microorganisms. This [bioengineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/bioengineering "Learn more about bioengineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technique takes the textile fabrics to the next level ([Rognoli et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib86)). Energy-harvesting technology can be incorporated into textiles to power [wearable electronics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wearable-electronics "Learn more about wearable electronics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [Internet of Things](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/internet-of-things "Learn more about Internet of Things from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IoT) gadgets. Such next-generation fabrics can capture and store energy by energy harvesting technology ([Satharasinghe et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib95), [Somkuwar et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib104)). Similarly, many technologies have been introduced for NGT, but sustainability is the prime concern ([Nishant et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib73)).

Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that [future textiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/future-textile "Learn more about future textiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are environmentally friendly. Despite many attempts to be environmentally friendly, some techniques of producing these fabrics can still lead to significant pollution. Nature can be harmed by the way we employ microscopic materials, fabric chemicals, and waste we produce when making these textiles. Another significant area for improvement with employing NGT is safety. Due to its versatility, the product may also introduce electrical dangers, chemical exposure, or heat generation. A difficult issue for advanced textiles is user privacy and security ([Kongahage and Foroughi, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib51), [Cherenack and Van Pieterson, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib16)).

Several reviews were done regarding the advanced and smart textiles. Cherenack et al. looked at the development, difficulties, design, embedded sensors, device connectivity, and illustrative technologies but should have mentioned the key aspects of it ([Cherenack and Van Pieterson, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib16)). Meanwhile Farooq et al. examined recent breakthroughs in wearable electronics, focusing on [data transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/data-transfer "Learn more about data transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), assistive robots, prosthetics, health monitoring, various wearables, and crucial insights regarding the development of electronic technology in the future without mentioning challenges ([Farooq and Zhang, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib32)). In their article, Ramlow and his colleagues addressed the obstacles to widespread commercial production and discussed the developments in [electronic textile](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electronic-textile "Learn more about electronic textile from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technologies, energy sources such as energy-harvesting textiles, and [wireless power transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wireless-power-transfer "Learn more about wireless power transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Ramlow et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib84)). Despite the novelty of the technology, Yang et al. discovered that individuals were eager to embrace and employ smart materials and technologies in assistive technology as long as they were comfortable, aesthetically pleasing, and easy to use ([Yang and Moody, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib123)). In addition, Oliveira and her research team also presented an overview of [intelligent textiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/intelligent-textile "Learn more about intelligent textiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), covering fundamental ideas, classifications, a variety of applications, and an emphasis on important production materials, but not mentioning the cost related to it ([de Oliveira et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib74)). Additionally, Syduzzaman and his colleagues provided a thorough analysis of the advancement of wearable electronics, covering sports, business, healthcare, and more, but they should have mentioned the procedures or techniques used ([Syduzzaman et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib108)).

However, these review papers have not mentioned all the aspects of producing an NGT. This review paper discusses sustainability, functionalization, and other major aspects that are related to NGTs. Also, the obstacle associated with traditional textiles and the chronological development of the textile has illustrated that other review papers did not mention. Considering the research gap, this article demonstrates techniques like Nanotechnology, 3D printing, Recycling, Bioengineering, and so on that are implementable for next-generation textile formation with their challenges and recommendations to make it sustainable. Furthermore, there are a lot of challenges to transforming traditional textiles into advanced textiles, which are not comprehensively referred to by existing review articles. Despite that, probable challenges associated with the transformation of advanced textiles from traditional textiles have been mentioned here. The researchers need to be concerned about the cost, scalability, safety concerns, consumer behavior towards the product, and environmental impact before going through the path. The author has discussed future prospects regarding NGT, such as achieving more sustainability, [customization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/customisation "Learn more about customization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and safety features and functionality, in this review paper. This article aims to present a thorough analysis of current textile breakthroughs and trends to pave the way for future technological advancements of NGT by maintaining sustainability toward a greener world.

Textile advances in sustainable practices, innovative materials, and intelligent technology continue to shape the future of the textile business. Additionally, Conventional textiles have a number of urgent concerns, including their considerable environmental effect, production of wasteful practices, dependency on scarce resources, limited usefulness, lack of sustainability, and issues relating to chemical consumption, worker conditions, and consumer awareness. These obstacles demonstrate the need for a more innovative and ecological approach to [textile manufacture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/textile-processing "Learn more about textile manufacture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), usage, and disposal. By embracing technological advancements, implementing sustainable practices, and promoting awareness, the textile industry can lessen these problems and create a new opportunity for more responsible and forward-thinking textiles.

They used a substance from a type of cabbage to dye the cotton and make it change color based on pH. A review on recent trends in smart textiles Allish S *, Suthiksha. P, K M Pachiappan, R. Divya Sathiyam and Saniya A Department of Costume Design & Fashion, PSG College of Arts and Science, Coimbatore, India. World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews, 2024, 24(01), 1824–1828 Publication history: Received on 30 August 2024; revised on 08 October 2024; accepted on 11 October 2024 Article DOI: https://doi.org/10.30574/wjarr.2024.24.1.3103 Abstract Recent advancements in both domestic and international research have considerably enhanced the development of smart fibers and smart textile, which are now being increasingly integrated into the textile industry. Advanced textiles encompass five primary functions; sensors, data processing, actuators, storage and communication. These technologies must be effectively integrated into clothing, ensuring they meet essential requirements like comfort, durability, and resilience to routine maintenance. Designers engaged with traditional textiles might address challenges by integrating smart materials and re-evaluating their design methodologies to leverage the distinctive properties of these advanced materials. This review examines recent developments in Smart Textiles, emphasizing the materials and their recent trends. Keywords: Textile; Smart fibers; Data processing; Sensors; Market review 1. Introduction The term "Smart Textiles" encompasses a diverse domain of research and innovations aimed at enhancing the functionality and applicability of conventional fabrics. These textiles are characterized by their ability to interact with the environment or user and include fibers, filaments, and yarns, as well as woven, knitted, or non-woven structures.[1] Meena et al. defined smart textiles as fabrics crafted from advanced or responsive materials capable of detecting stimuli and facilitating information transmission. Consensus among scholars holds that smart textiles possess the ability to sense environmental conditions (sensing function), act upon them (actuating function), and adjust their behavior accordingly (adaptive function), with their complexity progressing through three generational stages. The first generation, termed passive smart textiles, includes materials that solely detect external stimuli. The second generation, known as active smart textiles, not only senses environmental stimuli but also responds to them. The third generation, referred to as advanced, very smart, or ultra-smart textiles, integrates the capabilities to perceive, respond to, and adapt to environmental changes [2]. 2. Materials 2.1. Smart fibers In tandem with advancements in innovative technology and science, the textile industry has experienced significant transformations in recent years. Textiles are no longer merely utilized for warmth, protection, and fashion. Instead, they now offer distinct properties and functionalities that adapt to external environmental changes through the incorporation of smart fibers, unique structures, or cutting-edge fabrication technologies. Unlike traditional fibers, World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews, 2024, 24(01), 1824–1828 1825 smart fibers not only provide added value but have also been modified for applications across diverse fields.These fibers come in two forms: passive smart fibers and interactive smart fibers 2.1.1. Passive smart fibers Smart fibers are characterized as fibers, yarns, or mats that possess specific functions independent of external stimuli, such as anti-bacterial, self-cleaning, or waterproof-breathable properties. The motivation to engage with the external environment is minimal. Nevertheless, the inherent properties or structures of these fibers, or the integration of functional components, likely endow them with varying degrees of intelligence. 2.1.2. Interactive smart fibers In contrast to passive smart fibers, interactive smart fibers—classified as a type of functional material—exhibit autonomous interactive behaviors tailored to specific requirements. These behaviors typically involvereceiving informationor stimuli and providing feedback. Interactive smart fibers possess the capability to adapt in response to external environmental signals, demonstrating a higher degree of responsiveness[4]. 2.2. Smart textile Smart textiles are advanced materials and structures that incorporate active control mechanisms to sense and respond to environmental stimuli. These textiles can exhibit substantial alterations in their mechanical properties—such as shape, colour, and stiffness—or in their thermal, optical, or electromagnetic characteristics, depending on the stimuli encountered. They integrate a variety of components, including sensors, actuators, and electronic devices, to function as complex systems. [5] Indeed, there is no universally accepted classification for smart textiles; they are typically categorized based on their functionality into three types: 2.2.1. First generation: passive smart textiles Passive smart textiles are designed solely to detect and report information regarding environmental conditions or stimuli. These textiles are equipped only with sensors. Examples include ultraviolet-protective clothing, plasma-treated garments, and fabrics embedded with optical sensors. 2.2.2. Second generation: active smart textiles The second generation, or active smart textiles, incorporates both sensors and actuators. These textiles can remember their shape and structure, exhibit color-changing properties, regulate temperature, repel water, absorb vapors, and heat up as needed. They represent a more sophisticated form of "smart" fabric. 2.2.3. Third generation: ultra-smart textiles The third generation, known as ultra-smart textiles, represents a significant advancement. These textiles can not only monitor various data types but also predict and adapt to external conditions without manual adjustments. Equipped with an integrated microcomputer, they function similarly to a brain. Examples include spacesuits, I-wear, sport jackets, musical jackets, and wearable computers[3]. 2.3. Functions of smart textiles Essentially, an intelligent suit can be characterized by five distinct functions: Sensors, Data Processing, Actuators, Storage, and Communication. Each function serves a specific purpose, though not every intelligent suit will encompass all these functions. The functions may be overt or inherently integrated into the material or design. Each function demands suitable materials and construction, ensuring compatibility withthe suit's fundamentalrequirements: comfort, durability, and resistance to standard textile maintenance practices. 2.3.1. Sensors The fundamental principle of a sensor is its ability to convert one type of signal into another that can be interpreted by a designated reader, whether that reader is a physical device or a human. In the case of physical devices, signals are predominantly converted into electrical forms. Textile materials, due to their extensive coverage of the body, serve as highly effective measurement tools. The bio-signals commonly referenced in the literature include: temperature; biopotentials such as cardiograms and myograms; acoustic signals from the heart, lungs, digestion, and joints; ultrasound for blood flow; motion related to respiration; humidity from sweat; and pressure related to blood. It is World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews, 2024, 24(01), 1824–1828 1826 important to note that this list is not exhaustive. Significant research is required to identify the optimal parameters for measuring specific bodily functions and to develop suitable algorithms for data interpretation. Currently, suits are available that can measure heart rate, respiration rate, temperature, motion, and humidity. These typically incorporate conventional sensors embedded within the fabric. However, there are emerging examples of textile sensors for monitoring heart rate, respiration, and motion that have already demonstrated promising results. 2.3.2. Data processing Advanced data processing is essential only when active processing is required. Currently, the primary challenge lies in the interpretation of data. Textile sensors have the potential to generate vast amounts of data, but the interpretation of this data is problematic due to several issues: significant signal variability between patients, the complex nature of analyzing both stationary and time-dependent signals, the absence of objective standard values, and the lack of understanding of the intricate interrelationships between parameters. Furthermore, the textile material itself lacks computing power, necessitating the use of electronic components. While these components are available in miniaturized and flexible forms and can be embedded in waterproof materials, their durability remains limited. Ongoing research aims to integrate active components into fibers, yet several practical challenges must be addressed before computing fibers become commercially viable, including resistance to washing, deformation, and establishing reliable interconnections. 2.3.3. Actuators Actuators react to stimuli generated by sensor functions, potentially following data processing. They enable movement, release substances, produce sound, and perform various other actions. Among the most well-known examples in this domain are shape memory materials, particularly shape memory alloys, which are available in filament form. Due to their responsiveness to temperature changes, shape memory materials are suitable for use as actuators and align well with the demands of smart textiles. To date, applications of shape memory alloys in textiles have been limited. Notably, the Italian company CorpoNove, in collaboration with d’Appolonia, has developed the Oricalco Smart Shirt. 2.3.4. Storage Smart suits frequently require substantial storage capacity. The storage of data or energy is paramount, with sensing, data processing, actuation, and communication all demanding energy, predominantly electrical power. Efficient energy management entails an optimal integration of energy supply and storage capabilities. Potential energy sources available to a garment include body heat (Infineon [8]), mechanical motion (such aselastic deformation of the fabrics or kinetic energy from body movement), and radiation (particularly solar energy). 2.3.5. Communication In the realm of intelligent textiles, communication manifests in various forms: it may be necessary within a single component of the garment, between the components themselves, or between the wearer and the garment to transmit commands or information, and vice versa. Currently, communication within the garment is facilitated either through optical fibers or conductive yarns. For instance, to develop a flexible textile display, the employment of optical fibers is particularly advantageous. France Telecom has successfully created prototypes, such as a sweater and a backpack, demonstrating these technologies[5]. 2.4. Application of smart textiles Gore-tex smart fabric ,Jacket, wearable motherboard, smart running shoes, biosensor underwear, snart bar, motion detecting pants, NASA aero gel jacket, the sensory baby vest, intelligent garment for fire fighters, fashion[7]. 2.5. Recent trends in smart textiles Recent technological advancements are outlined to demonstrate the potential of emerging enabling technologies in surmounting existing technological barriers. Additionally, innovative strategies for drug delivery systems, emphasizing the development of functional materials, are detailed to highlight groundbreaking topics poised to influence the future of textiles. 2.5.1. Wearable sensors Numerous wearable technologies have emerged through the integration of sensors and data-transmitting devices into textiles. This advanced category of wearable electronic systems is engineered to cater to innovative applications across World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews, 2024, 24(01), 1824–1828 1827 various consumer sectors, including fitness and healthcare, military, public safety, and sports. Unlike conventional bulky electronic devices, which are typically rigid, smart textiles represent a fiber-based, flexible system capable of enduring complex and severe deformations with remarkable stability. 2.5.2. Drug release systems Textiles serve as a crucial interface between the human body and its environment, covering most of the body's surface for the majority of the day. This fundamental observation implies that textiles could be utilized as comfortable systems for the delivery of active compounds or pharmaceuticals, either to the body or to the surrounding environment. The skin, which can span up to 2 square meters, presents a potential pathway for drug delivery[6]. 2.6. Market overview According to a US report by Venture Development Corporation, the smart textiles market was valued at $248 million in 2004 and $304 million in 2005, with expectations to reach $642 million by 2008, reflecting a 27% compound annual growth rate. By 2012, the market had surpassed $2.5 billion and was projected to exceed $8 billion by 2018, growing at a 17.7% CAGR. Wrist-wear led the market in 2012, with revenues from wristwatches and wristbands exceeding $850 million. Consumer applications held the largest market share, surpassing $2 billion, while enterprise and industrial applications are expected to grow the fastest. North America, particularly the U.S., remains the largest market, but the Asia-Pacific region, led by China, is forecasted to grow the most rapidly. Developing countries could see significant profits from smart textiles but will need increased research, funding, and technology to capitalize on this opportunity. 2.6.1. Market segmentation This industry is categorized according to the following application sectors: Consumer Products; Military and Homeland Defense/Public Safety Applications; Computing; Biomedical; Vehicle Safety and Comfort; and Others, including Logistics and Supply Chain Management, as well as Signage. Key geographic regions encompass North America, Asia-Pacific, Europe, and the Rest of the World. 2.6.2. Major PLAYERS Among the principal entities exerting significant influence in this sector are E. I. Du Pont De Nemours and Company, Intelligent Clothing Ltd., Interactive Wear AG, International Fashion Machines Inc., Kimberly-Clark Health Care, Milliken & Company, Noble Biomaterials Inc., Outlast Technologies Inc., QinetiQ North America, Royal Philips Electronics N.V., Toray Industries Inc., among others[3]. 3. Discussion Recent advancements in textile technologies, innovative materials, nanotechnology, and miniaturized electronics are enhancing the feasibility of wearable systems. However, the crucial determinant for user acceptance of these devices remains fit comfort. We assert that achieving this objective necessitates a focus on the mechanical resistance and durability of materials within the challenging environment presented by the human body and society. Moreover, we believe that the development of smart textiles demands a multidisciplinary approach, integrating expertise in circuit design, smart materials, microelectronics, and chemistry with a comprehensive understanding of textile manufacturing. 4. Conclusion Smart fibers and smart textiles, along with their diverse applications, represent significant research focal points in the contemporary textile and apparel industry, as well as pivotal trends for future development. These innovative textile materials serve as the foundation for high-tech functional textiles, enhancing quality of life, increasing product value, improving labor conditions, and addressing industrial needs. Their presence in the application market is poised to expand significantly. As a critical economic growth driver for the textile sector, this emerging industry remains in a developmental phase. However, with ongoing advancements in science and technology, alongside the dedication of researchers, smart fibers and textiles are set to profoundly transform our lives, ushering in remarkable changes. enriched_points.expanded_content: Active smart textiles use embedded sensors to detect environmental stimuli like temperature, light, and moisture, allowing them to adapt and respond dynamically for enhanced user interaction.
Temperature-regulating fabrics adjust heat retention and dissipation based on the wearer's body temperature, providing comfort in varying climates by actively heating or cooling the fabric.
Color-changing textiles utilize thermochromic or photochromic materials that alter their hue in response to temperature changes or light exposure, creating visually dynamic clothing and accessories.
